summary: Gruber examines the failure of COVID-19 exposure notification apps built on the Apple/Google Bluetooth system. Despite the technical privacy-preserving design, adoption has been too low to be useful — fewer than half of U.S. states even offer the apps, and most residents in participating states haven't activated them. Using New Jersey as a case study, he shows that out of 9 million residents and 800,000 COVID cases, only 1,046 users uploaded positive test results and just 1,894 received exposure alerts. He concludes the system is fundamentally flawed because only conscientious people use it, potentially creating a false sense of security.
key_points: Fewer than half of U.S. states offered COVID-19 exposure notification apps, and most residents in participating states never activated them
New Jersey's numbers reveal the futility: 600,000 app users out of 9 million residents, with only 1,046 positive test uploads against 800,000 cumulative cases
The system suffers from a self-selection problem — only people already conscientious about COVID exposure bother using the apps
The multi-step opt-in process (install app, then separately enter verification code after testing positive) further reduces participation
The apps may actually be counterproductive by giving users a false sense of security
money_quotes: I think the whole idea is fundamentally flawed.
The only people who are using them are people who are conscientious about COVID exposure in the first place.
The whole endeavor seems pointless, looking at these numbers.
If anything, it might be giving the users of these apps a false sense of security.

If you use one of these apps and are exposed to someone who later tests positive, the odds that that person both uses the app and will report their positive test result seem not just low but downright infinitesimal.
key_insight: COVID-19 exposure notification apps failed because they could only reach the already-cautious, making the system's effectiveness infinitesimal against a pandemic that spreads most through the incautious.
